questions.question:
Use the patterns given below to prove the following WITHOUT using mathematical induction.

(A) Observe the pattern:
   1 = 1
   1 + 3 = 4
   1 + 3 + 5 = 9
   1 + 3 + 5 + 7 = 16
   ...
Use this to prove that sum_{k=1}^{n} (2k - 1) = n^2.

(B) Use the result of (A) to prove that:
   sum_{k=1}^{n} k = n(n+1)/2.
questions.to_find: Closed-form proofs (without induction) that sum of first n odd numbers equals n^2, and that sum_{k=1}^{n} k = n(n+1)/2

understand.plain: Part (A) asks you to see why summing the first n odd numbers always gives n^2. The pattern holds because odd numbers are exactly the gaps between consecutive perfect squares: (k^2 - (k-1)^2) = 2k - 1. Part (B) then uses part (A) cleverly: pairing the sum 1+2+...+n with a rearrangement yields 2*S = n(n+1), so S = n(n+1)/2. Both proofs are purely algebraic — no induction allowed.

keywords.explain: Each odd number 2k-1 equals k^2 - (k-1)^2. When you sum k from 1 to n these differences telescope, leaving only the last term minus the first: n^2 - 0^2 = n^2.
Write S = 1+2+...+n. Write it again in reverse order. Adding corresponding terms gives (n+1) exactly n times, so 2S = n(n+1).

understand.visual_alt: A square grid of n rows and n columns showing that the L-shaped gnomon at step k has exactly 2k-1 cells, so all gnomons together fill an n by n square.

given.aim: Prove sum_{k=1}^{n}(2k-1) = n^2 and deduce sum_{k=1}^{n} k = n(n+1)/2 without induction

terms.meaning: k^2 - (k-1)^2 = 2k - 1 for every positive integer k
terms.connects: Lets us rewrite each odd number as a telescoping difference

terms.meaning: A sum where most intermediate terms cancel, leaving only the first and last.
terms.connects: Used to close part (A) without induction

terms.meaning: Adding a forward and backward copy of the same sum makes each pair equal to n+1.
terms.example: 1+n = n+1, 2+(n-1) = n+1, ..., n+1 = n+1: n pairs each summing to n+1.
terms.connects: Converts sum_{k=1}^{n} k into the product n(n+1)/2 using part (A)

given.plan: For (A): rewrite 2k-1 = k^2-(k-1)^2, sum both sides, and observe telescoping. For (B): write S in two ways and use 2S = sum of n copies of (n+1).

steps.title: Key algebraic identity
steps.apply: Expand: k^2 - (k^2 - 2k + 1) = 2k - 1. This holds for every integer k >= 1.
steps.note: This single identity is the engine for part (A); no induction is needed.

steps.title: Prove part (A): telescoping sum
steps.note: Every intermediate squared term appears once with a plus sign and once with a minus sign, so they cancel.

steps.title: Split the odd-number sum to involve 1+2+...+n
steps.apply: Linearity: sum(2k-1) = 2*sum(k) - sum(1) = 2S - n. From part (A) this equals n^2.
steps.note: This rearranges to 2S = n^2 + n = n(n+1).

steps.title: Conclude part (B)
steps.apply: From 2S = n(n+1), divide both sides by 2: S = n(n+1)/2.
steps.note: This is the Gauss formula; the proof used only algebra and the telescoping result from part (A).

solution.result: Part (A): sum_{k=1}^{n}(2k-1) = n^2 (by telescoping). Part (B): sum_{k=1}^{n} k = n(n+1)/2 (by splitting the odd sum).

questions.formula_note: The proof rests on the single identity 2k-1 = k^2 - (k-1)^2, which turns the sum of odd numbers into a telescoping collapse.

questions.question:
Let P = C(k,0) + C(k+1,1) + C(k+2,2) + ... + C(2k,k)  and  Q = C(2k+1, k+1), where k is a positive integer. Then

A. P = Q + k   B. P = Q + k   C. P = Q   D. P = Q + 2k
questions.to_find: The relationship between P = sum_{i=0}^{k} C(k+i, i) and Q = C(2k+1, k+1)

understand.plain: P is a finite sum of binomial coefficients where the top and bottom both increase by 1 at each step: C(k,0), C(k+1,1), ..., C(2k,k). This is exactly the form handled by the Hockey Stick (Christmas Stocking) identity. Applying that identity directly gives P = C(2k+1, k+1). Since Q is defined as C(2k+1, k+1), we immediately get P = Q.

keywords.explain: sum_{j=r}^{n} C(j, r) = C(n+1, r+1). When the lower index r stays fixed and the upper index j runs from r to n, the sum collapses to one binomial coefficient.
C(n,k) = C(n, n-k). In particular C(2k+1, k+1) = C(2k+1, k), so both index forms give the same number.

understand.visual_alt: Pascal's triangle with a diagonal strip highlighted from C(k,0) down to C(2k,k), showing the accumulated sum points to C(2k+1,k+1) one step below and to the right.

given.aim: Evaluate P = C(k,0)+C(k+1,1)+...+C(2k,k) and compare with Q = C(2k+1,k+1)

terms.meaning: The i-th term (i = 0,1,...,k) is C(k+i, i) = C(k+i, k).
terms.connects: Matches the summation form of the Hockey Stick identity with fixed lower index r = k
Directly evaluates P as the single binomial coefficient C(2k+1, k+1)

terms.meaning: Q = C(2k+1, k+1), precisely the right-hand side of the Hockey Stick result.
terms.connects: Confirms P = Q without any further computation

given.plan: Rewrite the generic term of P as C(k+i, k), then apply the Hockey Stick identity sum_{i=0}^{k} C(k+i, k) = C(2k+1, k+1) = Q.

steps.title: Rewrite each term using symmetry
steps.apply: Since C(n,r) = C(n, n-r), we have C(k+i, i) = C(k+i, (k+i)-i) = C(k+i, k). So P = sum_{i=0}^{k} C(k+i, k).
steps.note: Rewriting with the fixed lower index k makes the hockey stick form obvious.

steps.title: Apply the Hockey Stick identity
steps.apply: Set r = k and j runs from k to k+k = 2k. So n = 2k. sum_{j=k}^{2k} C(j,k) = C(2k+1, k+1).
steps.note: The sum in P exactly matches this form with r = k and n = 2k.

steps.title: Compare with Q
steps.apply: Since Q is defined as C(2k+1, k+1) and we just showed P = C(2k+1, k+1), we conclude P = Q.
steps.note: Numerical check for k=2: P = C(2,2)+C(3,2)+C(4,2) = 1+3+6 = 10 = C(5,3) = Q. Confirmed.

solution.result: P = Q, so the answer is C.

questions.formula_note: The Hockey Stick identity sum_{j=r}^{n} C(j,r) = C(n+1,r+1) directly collapses the entire sum P into the single value Q = C(2k+1,k+1).

questions.question: The value of sum_{k=0}^{10} C(10,k) * 2^k is _________.

understand.plain: This sum looks like a mix of binomial coefficients and powers of 2. The Binomial Theorem tells us that (x + y)^n = sum_{k=0}^{n} C(n,k) * x^(n-k) * y^k. If we set n=10, x=1, and y=2, the right-hand side becomes exactly our sum: sum_{k=0}^{10} C(10,k) * 1^(10-k) * 2^k. The left-hand side is then (1+2)^10 = 3^10 = 59049.

keywords.explain: (x+y)^n = sum_{k=0}^{n} C(n,k) x^(n-k) y^k. Substituting specific values for x and y turns this into a powerful identity for evaluating combinatorial sums.
keywords.example: Set x=1, y=1: sum C(n,k) = 2^n. Set x=1, y=2: sum C(n,k)*2^k = 3^n.

keywords.explain: Recognize that the given sum matches the binomial expansion pattern with n=10, x=1, y=2. Once recognized, the closed form is immediate.

understand.visual_alt: A row of Pascal's triangle for n=10 with each entry C(10,k) multiplied by 2^k, all adding up to 3^10 = 59049.

terms.connects: Our sum is exactly this template with n=10, x=1, y=2

terms.meaning: The number of terms is 11 (k = 0 to 10) and the binomial coefficient row is C(10, k).
terms.connects: Identifies which row of Pascal's triangle is in use

terms.meaning: Setting x=1, y=2 in (x+y)^n makes x^(n-k) = 1 for all k, leaving sum C(10,k)*2^k.
terms.example: Left side: (1+2)^10 = 3^10. Right side: sum C(10,k)*2^k. They are equal.
terms.connects: Turns an abstract sum into the single number 3^10

given.plan: Match the sum against the Binomial Theorem with n=10, x=1, y=2. Then evaluate (1+2)^10 = 3^10.

steps.title: Recognise the binomial template
steps.apply: Set n=10, x=1, y=2. Right side becomes sum_{k=0}^{10} C(10,k) * 1^(10-k) * 2^k = sum_{k=0}^{10} C(10,k) * 2^k, which is exactly the given expression.
steps.note: Since 1^(anything) = 1, the x-factor vanishes and the sum simplifies perfectly.

steps.title: Evaluate the left side
steps.note: The key insight is just recognizing which values of x and y to substitute.

questions.formula_note: Substituting x=1, y=2 in the Binomial Theorem converts the entire sum into the single evaluation (1+2)^10 = 3^10 = 59049.

formulas.name: Sum of the First n Squares
Telescoping Series
formulas.plain: sum (f(x) - f(x+1)) = f(1) - f(m+1) — middle terms cancel

formulas.name: Sum of the First n Positive Integers
Hockey Stick Identity (Theorem 4)
formulas.plain: sum_{j=r}^{n} C(j,r) = C(n+1, r+1)   (j = r to n)
